Portfolio performance reflects Broadleaf’s Growth Equity Composite, described more fully under the caption “Performance Disclosures.” You are urged to read that information in its entirety in connection with any evaluation of Broadleaf’s performance statistics. Growth stocks had a tough relative quarter, as the market punished many of the big winners of the past few years. This “momentum” unwind resulted in a tough quarter for the BGEP.

Market Review & Outlook

We have written more than usual this quarter, given the volatility of the market and the flip- flopping developments in the economy. The rapid change in daily news flow has us on our toes, however, we feel that it makes sense to be more gradual with portfolio changes to the BGEP, as we discern what is true and what is narrative. The market is best described as moody, and we get a new mood each day. We believe it is important to listen to what the market is saying, but when it is hard to get a clear signal, it doesn’t always pay to obsess over every movement.

Our goal for this update will be to report facts on the economy, as we have recently given our opinions on the current tech and political environment. We will try our best to avoid AI or politics but feel obliged to mention that we are more constructive than the polarizing news flow. While at risk of being boring, sometimes a more sober approach is warranted.

Note: In the last couple of days we have spent preparing these thoughts, multiple highly respected market strategists have increased their odds of a recession, showing just how fragile the current environment is. We would note that most of these recession-call probabilities still reside below 50%, but the pace at which they are ticking up is notable. In addition, 4/2/2025 has been marked, “Liberation Day” by President Trump, which might represent yet another drastic shift in market conditions.

Earnings

Q4 2024 Earnings were rather strong, as earnings were up 13% in aggregate for large caps. Earnings also beat expectations rather handily, outpacing analyst expectations by about 7%, despite weakness in more cyclically oriented sectors such as energy and industrials. However, as we know, the market doesn’t care about the past – but cares about what is next.

The first look at annual guidance for 2025 was solid, but perhaps a bit conservative, as company management teams warned about the uncertainty in the business outlook, primarily due to tariffs. It is very common for management teams to guide conservatively in the first quarter of each year, and then set their sights on beating these conservative expectations.

There’s no doubt that given the volatile environment, management teams may find extra prudence to be wise, and this has been reflected as full-year earnings estimates have come down a hair since the beginning of the year.

If we drill down into the quarterly earnings expectations for 2025, the 1 st quarter has taken the largest hit as investors expect that the volatility of the current environment will bleed through in the upcoming quarter. However, a strong back half of the year is still expected, suggesting that analysts and market strategists are still expecting the current situation to improve as the year progresses.

Summary: Strong, resilient expectations in 2025.

Employment

The key cog for the economy is typically employment. Federal layoffs have thus far been absorbed relatively well, as weekly jobless claims have remained relatively low. If companies begin to suspect that economic uncertainty will not be resolved quickly, they will likely begin more significant layoffs to defend earnings.

Most management teams know that it is hard to make long-term strategic decisions in a whiplash kind of environment. Given this, companies are not hiring at the rapid pace they once were, nor are they shedding many employees. Many companies are simply maintaining the status quo, and as such, employees are more content to stay at their current jobs. As we saw a few years ago in 2022, we are not likely to have a recession unless further cracks emerge in employment.

Summary: Stable

The Consumer

Consumer sentiment surveys hit the lowest levels since 2022, as consumers expect inflation to pick up again for a multitude of reasons. Many management teams affirmed that reinflation fears may be justified, with the prices paid for goods beginning to trickle up. These management teams also indicated that the consumer is selective, but they are still active. Who doesn’t love a good deal? The most recent economic data from last Friday, March 28th seemed to affirm these observations, with inflation trickling up month over month, and with actual consumer spending less depressed than how consumers are feeling.

Note: Consumer sentiment remains well below pre-pandemic levels. Consumers HATE inflation.

Note: Actual spending is as steady as it gets, save for the 2020 craziness.

One of our favorite economists, Ed Yardeni, likes to point out that consumers love to spend, especially when they are depressed, so long as they remain employed. While surveys have value, it is important to watch what consumers do, not just listen to what they say.

Consumers have remained relatively depressed ever since the COVID pandemic, with actual consumer spending looking rather resilient. The latest data suggests a slight bump in the road, but no major trend change at this point. It is important to listen to how consumers feel, as it can indicate future weakness, but this has not been a very reliable signal over the past few years.

Summary: Watch What I Do, Not What I Say.

GDP

The Atlanta Fed puts out a forecast on real GDP growth expectations for the current quarter. This forecast has a pretty strong reputation as being a reliable predictor of GDP growth for the current quarter. The most recent quarterly Real GDP forecast is expected to shrink by 1.8%, which is certainly not a good sign. However, a deeper dive into the components of this figure shows that net exports are solely responsible for the decline. Put in plain English, the economy is still growing, but companies are importing cars, electronics, and a whole host of goods before tariff day, which is leading to negative Q1 growth estimates. Companies will not likely import at such a rapid rate once tariffs are in place, making a Q2 bounce possible, but hard to predict.

If you are in the market for a new car, it might not make sense to wait much longer.

Summary: Declining. Tariff pull-forward clouds data.

Uncertainty:

Economic policy uncertainty is near levels seen during COVID and during the Great Recession. This is surprising, as economic panic felt more palpable out on Main Street during those two prior peaks. While the current environment has raised a few eyebrows, we think that the uncertainty brought upon by tariffs and DOGE can be resolved rather quickly, for better or worse. We have no illusions that tariffs are good for consumers, but we do hope for more straightforward messaging and policy in due time.

It definitely seems like the business environment is on high alert right now, as M&A has slowed considerably. One of our favorite investing parables is, “Risk is where uncertainty is growing.” We won’t discount this one, but also think that self-inflicted dramas are more easily placated. In the meantime, risk is certainly elevated.

Summary: There’s a lot of fear out there.

Recently I have noticed in personal conversations and on social media that a debate has sparked on the wisdom of buying this dip. Many are happily buying it, throwing their hard-earned cash in potential harm’s way while other investors take risk off the table, fearing the worst is yet to come. This debate gets testy at times, as some investors question whether we can get yet another “V-shaped bounce.

Our crystal ball is as good as anyone else’s - and they are all pretty poor -, but we believe that both of these investors could be right. Being in the business of predicting recessions is tough and usually quite unprofitable work. We would remind folks that not long ago, it seemed like all of the smartest economists were calling for a recession that never came. Bear markets and volatility are a certainty of life, and they are the price we pay for the good times that will also no doubt, one day come.

A chart in my office shows the history of the US Economy. It serves as a reminder that we made it through a Civil War, the worst recession in US history (so bad it was called a Depression), two World Wars, a Cold War, the brink of nuclear catastrophe, endless panics, inflationary and deflationary regimes, and countless other periods of high uncertainty, just like we have today. I can also tell you that investors with a long-time horizon are happy with the forward returns from each of these uncertainty “peaks”. There is not much of a reason to think that this time will be different.

The real answer to the buy the dip question may be more of an emotional one. We feel that this could just be One Of Those Times, not inherently from a good or bad economy, but policy-induced anxiety. The solution to anxiety during these kinds of periods is usually altering one’s asset allocation – or buckling up for the ride.

We are coming off a long-term winning streak, and investors who began this run with us may find themselves today in a completely different financial situation than when it started. All the facts and charts in the world probably won’t change how you feel about your money, but we are still here to help. Our financial advisory team is one of the best in the business at managing the less quantitative, but more emotional intersection of behavioral investing and portfolio construction. Please do not hesitate to reach out to them if you have questions, or to me if you want to talk about some more epic charts and data!

About the Author

Pete is an Associate Portfolio Manager at Broadleaf, with a strong background in the financial services industry. He joined the firm in 2021, and has a proven track record of analyzing portfolio companies and identifying growth opportunities. Pete is a CPA and received his Chartered Financial Analyst( CFA®) designation in 2024. Pete lives in Hudson, Ohio with his wife Nicole.

Investment Style

The Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio employs a concentrated growth style of investing, holding approximately 25-35 equity positions from a cross section of economic sectors. Morningstar would classify us as a large cap growth manager, but we will invest in select small and midsize companies as unique opportunities avail themselves. Sector exposures are strongly influenced by our views on three determinants of investment value, which we define as the economic cycle, the innovation cycle, and the credit cycle. Individual securities are ultimately selected on the basis of their long-term growth potential, profitability, and intrinsic value as measured by their free cash flow generating characteristics. Innovative new ideas and themes are of particular interest.

Investment Objective

The portfolio’s goal is to provide equity like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle, utilizing a growth oriented investment style. The portfolio is suitable for investors seeking an exposure to a concentrated investment style which may be more volatile than the market as a whole. Investors should consider it as a portion of their investment portfolio within the context of their overall asset allocation and related investment goals.

The U.S. Dollar is the currency used to express performance.

$100,000. Historical results have not been updated retroactively to reflect changes in account minimums, but are reflected on a going forward basis.) To be included in the composite, an account must have been under management for at least one full quarter. If a significant cash flow in an underlying composite account during the quarter causes it to deviate from our intended growth style, we will remove the account for the period in which the significant cash event occurred. A significant cash flow is currently defined as 10% or more.

Total firm assets at quarter end were $462 million. Prior to January 5 th, 2006 the firm did not have any investment advisory clients. As a result, composite data prior to March 31 st, 2006 only reflects the performance of Doug MacKay’s personal retirement account.

The S&P 500 Index has been used for comparative benchmark purposes because the goal of the stated strategy is to provide equity-like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle. The S&P 500 is a broad based index reflecting the performance of the equity market in general. The S&P 500 Index is based on total returns which includes dividends. We monitor the performance of our growth style of investing by comparing our results to those of other large cap growth peers. While we believe these are appropriate benchmarks to use for comparison purposes, it should be expected that the volatility of the Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio may be higher due to its concentrated nature.

data.title: 2 REITs To Buy Before They Get Bought Out
data.description:
Over the past year, I have written many articles highlighting the opportunities present in the REIT sector (VNQ).

Their share prices have crashed even as their cash flows and dividends kept on growing. As a result, they are now trading at decade-low valuations:

It is not unusual to find REITs trading at 20, 30, or even 40% discounts relative to the value of the real estate they own, net of debt.

And because valuations are so low, we have now increasingly many REITs selling assets to unlock value and buy back shares.

There is also growing interest from major private equity players like Blackstone (BX) in taking REITs private to unlock value. Blackstone alone has bought out over $30 billion worth of REITs in the last few years, and they are determined to continue as long as REITs remain discounted.

Smaller investors like us can profit from this by buying shares in undervalued REITs that are partaking in such M&A activity to unlock value.

In what follows, I will highlight two such opportunities in today's market. The first one is selling assets to buy back shares. The second one is a likely buyout target:

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA; OTCPK:BSRTF): Selling Assets to Unlock Value

BSR REIT is an apartment REIT that focuses on the rapidly growing Texan Triangle:

The REIT has long traded at a steep 30% discount to its net asset value, and the management has been buying back shares with its retained cash flow to unlock value.

But the management appears to be finally running out of patience.

The REIT just announced its biggest deal since its IPO in 2018.

They are selling a $618 million dollar portfolio, representing roughly 30% of their assets to AvalonBay Communities (AVB) at their net asset value. This resulted in a $111 million profit, representing about $2 per share.

We have long argued that BSR REIT was undervalued, trading at a steep discount to its net asset value, and that the management was well-aligned with shareholders and likely to force upside realization if the market failed to close the valuation gap.

Therefore, this deal strongly validates our investment thesis.

It is very beneficial for four reasons:

1) Validation of the NAV: It validates the NAV of the company, which is about 40% higher than its current share price. This NAV is based on a 5.2% cap rate, and BSR sold these assets at a slightly lower 5.1% cap rate despite having weaker growth prospects than the rest. I was in contact with their investor relations this morning, and they told me that they are selling these assets because they have fully benefited from their management platforms, implying that there is little upside left for value-add. Also, looking at the pre-and post-transaction portfolio, we find that the average rent, occupancy rate, age, etc., remain the same, which confirms that they didn't just sell their best assets:

2) Lower Leverage: From the table, you can also see that they expect this transaction to reduce their leverage from 46% to 38%. This should reduce the concerns about how rising interest rates could impact the company in the coming years.

3) Accretive Capital Recycling: The management has a great track record at selling assets and redeploying the proceeds accretively into new opportunities. This is a key reason behind the company's significant outperformance in recent years, and they expect to do more of the same following this transaction. It gives them significant liquidity to pursue new accretive acquisitions.

4) Increased Odds of an Eventual Buyout: With this deal, the management proved that they are willing and able to sell substantial parts of the company if the market doesn't recognize its fair value. This should have already been clear from the significant share buybacks, insider purchases, and high insider ownership, but this deal reaffirms it once again. The company's investor relations also told me that this deal will eliminate 15 million Class B shares of legacy holders, removing their "voting/veto" rights. These legacy holders may, at times, have different motivations and tax positions, so eliminating them will put them in a better position to maximize value going forward. This seems to hint at a potential buyout or more similar transactions in the future if the market fails to recognize the fair value of the shares. It gives them optionality.

So, overall, this should greatly improve the company's market sentiment.

The REIT has grown the fastest in its peer group in recent years, and yet, it still trades at the lowest valuation and a significant discount to its net asset value:

This transformational deal should help it close the gap, potentially leading up to 40% upside.

Rent growth should also strongly accelerate in 2026, further helping it close the gap.

Finally, the market expects two interest rate cuts later this year, which should also help its stock.

While we wait for the upside, we earn a 4.3% tax-advantaged dividend yield (classified as return of capital), paid on a monthly basis, and the company will keep creating further value for us.

Healthcare Realty Trust Incorporated (HR): The Next Buyout Target?

Healthcare Realty is the biggest REIT focusing on medical office buildings.

This property sector is today doing very well, benefiting from the rapidly aging population and relatively little new supply, resulting in growing occupancy rates and rents:

Yet, because "medical office building" has the word "office" in it, the market appears to have panicked, causing all these REITs to sell off in association with other office REITs.

As a result, the valuation of the REIT is now historically low, trading at an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value and just around 10x funds from operations, or FFO:

Current P/FFO Historic P/FFO
Healthcare Realty 10.5x 15-20x

There is a clear mismatch between the fundamentals and the share price of the company.

The share price crashed, and it was as if HR's properties were facing severe challenges. But in reality, the REIT has guided to grow its same property NOI by 3% annually, and the occupancy rate for its properties is rising as a result of the undersupply:

But despite this strong performance, they still trade at an estimated 30% discount to their net asset value.

Just like BSR REIT, they have been readily selling assets in order to buy back shares to help unlock value for shareholders.

However, this hasn't had a major impact on the share price so far, and this recently motivated the activist investor Starboard Value to acquire a 6% stake in the REIT.

They are now in the process of revamping the management and board.

The company's CEO, CFO, and some board members have already stepped away.

The REIT has also signed a cooperation agreement with Starboard Value, and they have brought in new board members who are highly experienced in REIT M&A. A good example is the former CEO of VEREIT, which sold itself to Realty Income (O) at a significant premium.

My expectation is that Starboard will put all cards on the table.

If they still fail to earn a more reasonable valuation, I expect them to push for an M&A transaction.

HR already has a great relationship with the private equity powerhouse KKR & Co. (KKR). They have done many JV deals with them, so a KKR buyout would be one clear option.

Another option would be to approach a large diversified healthcare REIT like Welltower (WELL). They trade at a large premium relative to HR and could offer a significant premium, but the deal would still be highly accretive to them.

It remains unclear what path they will choose, but one thing is clear: the stock is cheap.

We estimate fair value at 16x FFO, which would unlock nearly 50% upside from today's level.

The company also offers a 7.7% dividend yield, but I caution investors against buying it just for the high yield. The dividend payout ratio is high at over 100%, and given that the company is going through major changes, it wouldn't surprise me if they decided to cut the dividend to focus on paying off debt and buying back shares.

It could lead to near-term volatility but would likely benefit shareholders over the long run.

Closing Note

At High Yield Landlord, we have consistently profited from various REIT M&A transactions, and we expect many more later this year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DINT ETF: Outperforming International Equity ETF Is Worth A Look
data.description:
What is DINT?

The Davis Select International ETF (DINT) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in a concentrated portfolio of global businesses principally outside the U.S. selected using the Davis Investment Discipline. The Fund’s investment objective is long-term growth of capital. Davis Advisors, the parent company of the fund, pitches that they can tap into the revenue and profit growth potential of companies in that are domiciled outside the U.S. and invest in ~30 companies that will outperform the MSCI ACWI ex US Index benchmark. And over the last several years, the fund has done an incredible job and manages $207MM in assets.

I remain skeptical of many actively managed ETFs, and it's part of the rationale of why I'm continuing to write about which ETF's stack up better against their competitors. Often times, investors are trying to get exposure to a certain industry, but many times are faced with terrible options that underperform with high fees. This article will tell you whether it's worth investing with DINT's asset managers or whether you should just buy ACWX, an ETF that tracks the MSCI ACWI ex US Index, but is passively managed. That means it's fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus DINT's managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity. I've worked in capital markets credit risk in the fund business at one of the largest banks, and have noticed that more often than not, these actively managed ETFs are simply not worth the high fees - and the majority of the time, these fund managers underperform the passive market.

After further review, my analysis shows that DINT is actually one of the outperformers. The fund has materially beaten the benchmark over multiple medium and long term time frames since inception, while making good investments without tacking too many higher fees. I recommend investors buy DINT if they want to get exposure to the international equities - with a focus on China and Korea, as I'll get into below.

Fund Breakdown

FDG was launched on March 1, 2018 by Davis Advisors with a mandate to look for "businesses that possess characteristics that Davis Advisors believes foster the creation of long-term value, such as proven management, a durable franchise and business model, and sustainable competitive advantages". The fund really highlights management as a key factor, with the fund noting that "individual stock selection and... the ability to evaluate management is critical. Davis Advisors routinely visits managers at their places of business in order to gain insight into the relative value of different businesses."

The fund noted that they make their investment decisions based primarily on their fundamental analysis of individual companies that are undervalued, rather than on broad economic forecasts. The most important part of the fund, in my opinion, is that the fund targets a relatively limited number of companies, generally 30 securities, which, in my experience, is usually a death wish for an active manager over the long term.

However, these fund managers have done an incredible job. Upon further review of Davis Advisors, they are a mid-sized fund manager with over $25Bn in AUM and about 25 funds based in Arizona. They have beaten the passive benchmarks significantly in their active funds and have built a portfolio that is quite a bit different from the benchmark to generate alpha. Upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm impressed that the fund has actually achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past five years (since launch) showing significant performance upside vs. ACWX, the passive competitor. Amidst recent volatility, the fund has picked up steam vs. the passive peer.

The fund has gained on its benchmark over the past several years, while employing a 0.66% yearly ETF expense ratio for the fees to the asset manager. This is only slightly higher than ACWX's expense ratio of 0.32%, while seeing stronger returns over medium and long-term ranges.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing DINT's portfolio, there are significant differences in the portfolio breakdown. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio has various differences from the benchmark, with significant concentration in China and Korea, and a significant reduction in EU holdings. The fund has also skewed heavy in consumer discretionary and financials, which tells me that they have been picking smaller growth stocks with good management within the Index - and beating the benchmark. When a fund focuses on undervalued earnings growth and doesn't own any commodities, employs active management, and consistently beats the benchmark with a unique portfolio, my ears perk up.

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 1.14 which means that the fund will have more volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. The fund has seen some larger swings than the benchmark but has consistently remained an outperformer with lower fees. Additionally, the R-squared value that indicates how much of a fund’s fluctuations were attributable to movements in the fund’s benchmark index was 65/100, which means that the fund doesn't really track the index pretty closely. This has borne out with almost no mainland European holdings and significant concentration in China and Korea, with minor holdings in Canada, Japan, and Singapore.

Upon a deeper dive into management, I see no reasons to believe that outperformance can't continue. Danton Goei manages the fund and received his M.B.A. from The Wharton School. He was previously employed at Bain & Company, Morgan Stanley Asia Ltd. and Citigroup. Mr. Goei speaks multiple languages and has lived in Europe, Asia and currently resides in the U.S. Ultimately, on my review, I couldn't find any rationale as to why outperformance may not continue and if I had to pick someone to track international equity and evaluate strong management, Mr. Goei seems well qualified.

Risks & Conclusion

DINT is a well-run, actively managed ETF that continues to track and outperform its benchmark, the MSCI ACWI ex US Index. The fees of 0.66% are not too bad, and only a bit higher than passively managed options, and the fund has seen significant outperformance over the last 5 years. While the fund management may change, and global equities may increase in volatility due to tariff wars, if you're an investor who wants exposure to a basket of international equities, this fund is a good option.

data.title: A Historic Opportunity For Dividend Investors
data.description:
Over the past several years, AI stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR) have soared over growing market bullishness on AI's potential to completely transform the global economy. Meanwhile, many dividend stocks have been left behind, as evidenced by the significant underperformance of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD) relative to the S&P 500 (SP500)(SPY) and the Nasdaq (QQQ) since the beginning of 2023:

That being said, it seems a bit ironic to me that the market is bullish not only on AI but also on the underlying technology powering AI with companies like NVDA, as well as AI applications with stocks like PLTR, and yet is seemingly pricing in little to no benefit to the companies that will actually be the ones who pay companies like PLTR to use AI to unlock value in their businesses.

On top of that, sectors like REITs (VNQ) remain beaten down, especially triple net lease REITs like Realty Income (O), due to fairly elevated long-term interest rates and expectations that they will remain higher for longer due to sticky inflation. However, if AI is really going to fulfill the potential that the markets are currently pricing in for it to have, it should have massive deflationary impacts over the long term. This, in turn, should push interest rates lower and be a major tailwind for O and many other high-yielding dividend stocks. Additionally, there are some companies that are directly related to the AI boom that the market has largely overlooked right now. In this article, I will discuss some of my top picks that are positioned to benefit from this historic opportunity.

The Historic Investment Opportunities For Dividend Investors

I think that right now is a historic opportunity for dividend investors to take advantage of this once-in-a-generation technological revolution. I see two historic opportunities for dividend investors to take advantage of the AI boom to set their portfolios up for long-term total return outperformance.

The first one is, as I already alluded to, buying beaten-down dividend stocks that are positioned to benefit from AI applications, as well as the secondary impacts of those applications—namely, plummeting inflation, which should also drive interest rates lower. Companies that can invest and take advantage of this include O, as I already mentioned, as well as consumer products stocks that are currently attractively valued. Consumer products and industrials stocks are undervalued due to the fact that their supply chain, marketing, transportation, and production efficiencies should all be greatly enhanced by the mass application of advanced AI algorithms.

Companies in particular that look attractive right now include PepsiCo (PEP), as AI has enormous potential to unlock value in a company with such a large supply chain, production, distribution, and marketing channels; Amcor (AMCR), which is also deeply undervalued and has potential to grow rapidly even without accounting for the benefits of AI; and also Pfizer (PFE), whose stock price is severely beaten down, yet it has enormous potential to dramatically enhance the productivity of its R&D pipeline by implementing AI algorithms to improve drug development processes.

Of course, on the interest rate front, in addition to O, another REIT that looks very attractive at current prices is Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), as it trades at a meaningful discount to net asset value while having a strong balance sheet and being well positioned to benefit from a revival in the life sciences industry. AI algorithms should drive strong profitability through improved drug development efficiency for companies like PFE, which fill up ARE’s lab space.

Meanwhile, the other big opportunity to benefit from the AI boom in the dividend stock space is in energy infrastructure (MLPX). This is because AI will eventually be constrained by how much energy can be produced to power the data centers that will be needed to run these huge AI algorithms. While some energy producers like Vistra (VST) and Constellation Energy (CEG) have already been bid up aggressively, as have some midstream infrastructure companies like The Williams Companies (WMB), there are others that seem to have been relatively overlooked by Mr. Market.

Two in particular are run by Brookfield (BAM, BN), including Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP, BEPC) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP, BIPC). BEP has a very large power purchase agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) to power its data centers and is optimistic about winning additional contracts in the future. Despite that incredibly powerful growth tailwind, BEP currently trades at a very attractive valuation, with a distribution yield of well over 6% and the potential to grow its cash flows per unit at a double-digit pace for the foreseeable future.

Meanwhile, BIP has a diversified infrastructure portfolio that has the ability to benefit immensely from the AI boom, including its electricity production and its regulated utilities business, its natural gas and midstream infrastructure, and most of all, its data segment that includes a significant and rapidly growing data center development pipeline. Management expects this segment to generate 20%+ IRRs for the company moving forward, and it may eventually become its largest individual segment. On top of that, BEP currently offers a distribution yield of 5.7% and also expects to grow its cash flows per unit at a double-digit pace for the foreseeable future.

As such, it presents a similarly compelling opportunity to BEP.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, while the Nvidias and Palantirs of the world were the big winners in recent years for those betting big on AI, in our view the next chapter is likely to benefit other companies in the market that have thus far been overlooked—whether it be large companies that stand to benefit immensely from AI applications like PEP, AMCR, and PFE; real estate companies that stand to benefit from plummeting inflation and thereby lower interest rates, such as O and ARE; or AI infrastructure companies like BEP and BIP that have thus far been overlooked by Mr. Market. These are the sorts of opportunities we are looking for and loading up on as High Yield Investor.

data.title: Gilead: HIV Franchise Woes Highlight Cancer Follies
data.description:
Gilead (GILD) has worked for the longest time to reinforce its dominant HIV franchise with alternate revenue streams. With HIV funding possibly under current attack, the strategic wisdom of this effort is clear; unfortunately the challenges faced in this regard have been stark.

Gilead's HIV franchise netted $19.612 billion in revenues in 2024. Oncology, its next most productive franchise, came in a distant second at $3.289 billion (2023 and 2024 revenues stated in this article derive from Gilead's latest 10-K (the "10-K") p. 39).

Efforts to build a cancer franchise have been flawed

Overview

Gilead's longstanding efforts to build its cancer franchise have been particularly challenging as highlighted by my 10/2020 article "Gilead's Cancer Follies" ("Follies"). This effort fueled by billions of dollars in acquisitions over long years has underperformed drastically.

With dozens of deals done aggregating tens of billions in investments, the three biggest acquisitions offer contrasting pictures. The top two have netted several approved therapies, with solid prospects for more on the near horizon. The third has fizzled with no remaining pipeline traces.

Major cancer acquisitions >~$5 billion

Kite 08/2017

Gilead's foray into cell therapy began with its 08/2017 ~$12 billion acquisition of Kite Pharma. As described in Follies, the FDA approved its first medicine, axi-cel in treatment of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma [DLBCL]; it received FDA approval under the trade name YESCARTA, in short order on 10/2017.

Subsequently, in 07/2020 the FDA approved Tecartus (brexucabtagene autoleucel, formerly KTE-X19) for the treatment of adult patients with relapsed or refractory mantle cell lymphoma [MCL]. In 2024, these two generated aggregate revenues of $1.973 billion, up ~ 6% from $1.869 billion in 2023.

Unfortunately, as described by CCO Mercier in Gilead's 02/2025 Q4, 2024 earnings call (the "Call") its cell therapy business faces upcoming headwinds:

... the fourth quarter cell therapy sales of $488 million were flat on a sequential basis and up 5% year-over-year, reflecting the continued challenging competitive dynamics in the U.S. and in Europe. These headwinds include a number of new cell therapy launches across indications both in the U.S. and outside the U.S. in addition to a slower than targeted uptake of cell therapy as a class.

In my 03/2024 article "Gilead's Business Development: The Good, The Bad, And The Spinning Kite Solution" ("Spinning Kite") I floated the idea that Gilead should free itself from its Kite albatross. I characterized Kite's technology as clunky; it has needed constant updating with expensive BD. I believe it has turned into a long-term experiment with no clear profit timeframe in sight.

Forty Seven Inc. 03/2020

Gilead's acquisition of Forty Seven for $4.9 billion added magrolimab to its pipeline; magrolimab is a monoclonal antibody that was in clinical development for the treatment of several cancers. Magrolimab's development has been star crossed.

Initially, it was anticipated to serve as a pipeline in a molecule. The following graphic from 06/2022's "Gilead's Future In 3 Molecules" shows Gilead's elaborate magrolimab development plans:

As famously penned by Robert Burns in 1785:

The best-laid schemes o' mice an' men / Gang aft agley, / An' lea'e us nought but grief an' pain, / For promis'd joy!

Such was the case with Gilead's grand plans for magrolimab. In 02/2024 Gilead announced it would not pursue further development of magrolimab in hematologic cancers. It then announced it was pausing enrollment in its solid tumor trials.

Immunomedics 09/2020

Gilead's acquisition of Immunomedics for $21 billion added Trodelvy (sacituzumab govitecan-hziy), a first-in-class Trop-2 directed antibody-drug conjugate [ADC] to its pipeline.

In 02/2023 the FDA approved Trodelvy as a second line treatment for certain breast cancer patients. In the Call Gilead reported:

...fourth quarter Trodelvy sales of $355 million increased 19% year-over-year, and 7% sequentially with full year sales increasing 24% year-over-year to $1.3 billion. Year-over-year growth for both periods reflected higher demand in all regions, highlighting the important role of Trodelvy in metastatic breast cancers, including in second line metastatic triple negative breast cancer, where it's a standard-of-care.

In terms of financial return the Immunomedics deal is not looking good; its full year 2024 revenues were $1.315 billion; 2023 revenues were $1.063 billion.

Green shoots

In 12/2024 Trodelvy was granted breakthrough therapy designation as a second line treatment for adult patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer (ES-SCLC). Gilead is advancing Trodelvy in multiple late stage clinical trials as reflected on Q4, 2024 earnings presentation (the "Presentation") slide 22 below:

In Spinning Kite, I reviewed a laundry list of Gilead's oncology BD over recent years. Included were three deals with Arcus (RCUS):

  1. 05/27/2020 - $175 million upfront plus a $200 million equity investment deal for immediate rights to zimberelimab and opt-in rights to all other RCUS candidates contingent on the payment of fees ranging from $200 - $275 million per program;
  2. 11/18/2021 - $725 million option exercise for three Arcus programs under 05/2020 agreement, including Domvanalimab an anti-TIGIT antibody in Phase 2 and Phase 3 studies in non-small cell lung cancer [NSCLC];
  3. 01/30/2024 - increased its interest from around 20% to 33% by buying $320 million of shares to accelerate development programs.

This Arcus deal may yet pan out for Gilead. Domvanalimab is listed in dark blue on slide 22 above along with Trodelvy as a featured molecule with a promising future. During the call, CMO Berger provided somewhat dour reflections on Domvanalimab when he observed:

...I've always believed TIGIT is an interesting immuno-oncology target. There is potential for differentiation given the fact that domvanalimab has the FC silent design. There were some encouraging early Phase 2 data like the ARC-7, the ARC-10 study, the EDGAR gastric study. But those were, of course, small early dataset. So again, we are awaiting the data. There are two ongoing Phase 3 studies, STAR-121 and STAR-221, which really will define our path forward there.

Presentation slide 37 below provides an overview of Gilead's cell therapy pipeline:

With three Phase 3 programs, this shows promise of potential ongoing growth, subject to caveats about upcoming headwinds.

Gilead's guidance for 2025 is restrained and fails to reflect possible HIV funding challenges

Presentation slide 32 below sets out Gilead's guidance for 2025:

Its total guided midpoint revenues of $28.4 billion for 2025 are slightly below its actual 2024 revenues of ~$28.7 billion and well above its ~$27.1 billion revenues in 2023. GAAP diluted EPS for 2025 at a midpoint of $6.15 had me stumped insofar as the 10-K (p. 38) showed GAAP diluted EPS for 2024 as $0.38 down 92% from 2023.

The 10-K attributes this difference to:

A pre-tax in-process research and development (“IPR&D”) partial impairment charge of $4.2 billion related to Trodelvy IPR&D assets; and • Higher acquired IPR&D expenses, primarily $3.8 billion related to the acquisition of CymaBay; partially offset by • Higher product sales; and • Lower income tax expense.

Overall, Gilead's guidance struck me as neutral. The concern I have as a shareholder goes back to its HIV franchise. In its 10-K outlook it expects:

...continued increases in demand for our products overall, bolstered by the growth of our HIV business.

If funding support for its HIV business gets nipped this could have a significant impact on Gilead's 2025 and subsequent years' overall results.

Conclusion

Ironically, Gilead's fortunes could be impacted positively if programs designed to reduce the spread of HIV are cut as has been suggested. On the other hand, cutting funding for programs that help fund HIV therapies is an unambiguous negative for Gilead.

The market hates uncertainty. As a Gilead shareholder, I hate uncertainty impacting its HIV business. We currently are in an environment where the news cycle is dishing out gobs of uncertainty impacting Gilead's HIV business. Accordingly, I rate Gilead as a "Hold" with a caveat that it could be unusually volatile over the near and mid-term.

It is comforting for shareholders to hear Gilead's dividend policy, as stated in the Call:

For 2025, we announced today a 2.6% increase in our quarterly cash dividend to $0.79 per share, and we remain committed to growing our dividend over time.

Its quant dividend grades are all solid, ranging from A- to B-.

data.title: Chipotle: Premium Valuation, Solid Growth Figures
data.description:
Chipotle Mexican Grill (NYSE:CMG) has been growing rapidly over the past few years. The growth shows no sign of stopping, and Bill Ackman just increased his stake by 17% to nearly 29 million shares. He bought the stock for the first time in 2016, and it was a smart move. Since then, the stock has risen 527%. Bill Ackman makes smart investment choices, but his temperament doesn’t always help. For example, he sold Netflix shares in 2022, the same year he bought the stock, and lost $400 million on a $1.1 billion investment (a loss of about 36%). The reason: Netflix was losing subscribers.

I am surprised that Bill Ackman bought Chipotle stock after having a great return. He is clearly very bullish on the company. A quick look leads me to conclude that the stock is expensive at a P/E of 40. However, the recent pullback makes the stock attractive. Loop Capital sees an opportunity and upgrades the rating to Buy, seeing opportunities due to strong revenue growth despite economic challenges. The tariffs imposed by Donald Trump are not expected to have much of an impact on earnings, as only 2% of purchases come from Mexico (avocados). The bullishness surrounding the stock and the recent purchase by Bill Ackman give me reason to investigate the stock further.

What makes Chipotle unique from its competitors is that it owns its restaurants. It’s not a franchise model like McDonald's. This gives Chipotle complete control over the customer experience and profit margins. Chipotle also invests in drive-trus and digital ordering, which increases the efficiency of the company. I also see a nice growth opportunity: Chipotle can grow significantly through international expansion.

Strong Financial Results

The results for the full year 2024 are excellent. Sales increased by almost 15% to $11.3 billion. The comparable sales growth of 7.4% was mainly achieved by an increase in the number of customers (only 2.1% price increases). This shows that more and more people are discovering Chipotle and are satisfied with their menu. Profitability also increased to 16.9% (in 2023: 15.8%), mainly due to the increased restaurant margins of 26.7%. A notable point: the restaurant margin decreased slightly in Q4 due to more expensive avocados and higher labor costs. Chipotle was able to compensate for this by slightly raising prices. As a result, adjusted net income per share rose to $1.12, an increase of 24.4% compared to 2023. 2024 was another strong year.

The outlook also looks good. For 2025, Chipotle expects comparable sales growth of approximately 3-5%. It also wants to open 315 to 345 new locations, more than 80% of which will have a Chipotlane drive-tru. This expansion will be financed from its own cash flows. In 2024, the operating cash flow amounted to $2.1 billion, enough to provide for expansion. The balance sheet is very strong because Chipotle has no long-term debt and has $2.2 billion in cash and marketable securities. This means that interest rate increases do not have a direct impact on the operating results. It is possible that during rate increases, consumers have less to spend and are therefore less inclined to go to restaurants. Chipotle has not experienced this in recent years.

A Huge Market Potential

Chipotle mainly serves Mexican food, which makes it popular among Latinos in the US. In my previous article (about Constellation Brands) I wrote that Trump's import tariffs are aimed at combating illegal immigration. For Chipotle, this means that the Latino population will probably grow less quickly. This could be an obstacle to further growth for Chipotle. I don't know what percentage of Chipotle's customers are Latino. However, I do see this as a potential (small) long-term risk, as the total customer base could grow with international expansion.

Chipotle has seen a rapid increase in popularity worldwide recently. The dotted line is the expected popularity (Google doesn’t have all the data yet). And that’s a good thing because popularity hasn’t increased since 2016. An increase in popularity can lead to more potential customers.

Chipotle is a fast food restaurant, like McDonald's, Taco Bell, and KFC, and has a strong growth rate with high-growth potential. International expansion is a huge opportunity, and the Chipotlanes will significantly improve efficiency per restaurant. With a strong debt-free balance sheet, Chipotle has plenty of room to expand and has the potential to become as big as McDonald's.

Reinvesting Yields More

McDonald's, Yum! Brands (Taco Bell and KFC), and Restaurant Brands (Tim Hortons, Burger King, etc.) all pay dividends while Chipotle is still in its growth phase. Instead, the company buys back its own shares. In 2024, it bought back nearly $1 billion worth of shares. At the current market capitalization, this equates to a buyback yield of 2.5%. There is still $1 billion open for future buybacks. This strategy increases earnings per share and the stock price, as there is more demand. Chipotle may not be attractive to dividend investors, but one must remember that the profitability of opening new restaurants is strong and justifies reinvestment (instead of paying dividends).

The stock valuation is substantial, with a forward P/E ratio of 40. On the other hand, several analysts expect an increase in earnings per share of around 15% this year.

Analysts expect the strongest growth for Chipotle compared to other companies in the table. Personally, I find Restaurant Brands more interesting when I just look at the table. With an expected growth that is slightly lower (12% earnings per share growth over 2025), and an attractive stock valuation and dividend, this is certainly an interesting stock to take a closer look at. Geographically, however, the growth opportunities for Restaurant Brands are more limited than for Chipotle.

Conclusion

Chipotle is a well-run company that has managed to achieve strong growth despite high inflation. With a strong balance sheet without long-term debt and high profit margins, this is the restaurant chain par excellence to invest in. Chipotle can expand internationally, which offers further growth opportunities. The new import duties have hardly any impact on its profits, but I do expect that the Latino population will grow less quickly in the long term. How much impact this will have on Chipotle’s bottom line is still the question, I expect the effect to be small because of the international expansion. The share valuation is substantial compared to the sector average, and that is actually something that prevents me from investing. Chipotle wants to open at least 315 new locations by 2025, the growth ambitions are high and that somewhat justifies the expensive share valuation. If Chipotle continues to grow in the coming years, the recent decline would be a good time to get in.

Elevated levels of market volatility continued during Q1. US stocks underperformed their international peers. Our portfolio was largely immune from the market volatility during Q1 due to our international diversification, as well as some stock-specific investments that continue to work.

Our largest contributors to performance were Haypp, Marex (MRX), Mr. Cooper (COOP) and Brookdale Senior Living (BKD). Our biggest detractors were Distribution Solutions Group (DSGR), X-Fab Silicon Foundries (OTCPK:XFABF) and AAR Corp (AIR), which were impacted by overall market volatility but still hold strong investment theses in the long run.

Portfolio

Night Watch manages a global value strategy that differentiates on the following points:

Catalyst – We predominantly buy value companies with an identifiable catalyst for a rerating. Catalysts can include industry tailwinds or company specific catalysts (e.g., earnings inflection, CEO changes, refinancing).

Inside Ownership – We aim to find companies where management has considerable ownership in the company. We consider this alignment of interest to be an important determinant of share price performance.

Unique Names – To differentiate from a long list of other value strategies, we seek unique portfolio holdings that have little overlap with a typical wealth management portfolio. We thus aim to provide our LPs with diversification from their other investments in addition to strong performance.

Strategy Update

In previous quarters we presented our portfolio in terms of various themes to which we are seeking exposure. Those themes remain relevant, but in this letter, we want to dive deeper into the strategy behind our global diversification. In broad terms, we have a basket with US stocks, European stocks and Hong Kong-listed stocks.

USA

The S&P500 (SP500, SPX) is the most expensive developed market globally. At a P/E multiple of 21.3x, it trades roughly 1.5 standard deviations above its 20 year mean. The following graph shows the expected future return per year over the subsequent 10-year, as a function of P/E multiple. If history is any guide, S&P500 investors should expect to earn anywhere between -1% to 3% per year for the next 10 years.

Fortunately, there are many US small and mid-caps that trade at reasonable valuations and have improving businesses as well as good management teams. This is where we focus our attention.

The high valuations in the US for the last years were justified by higher economic growth compared to the rest of the world. This seems to be changing.

The new Trump administration is expected to make various policy changes that are negative for stocks, such as the introduction of tariffs and a decrease in government spending. At the same time, it promises policy changes that could boost stock prices such as deregulation, corporate tax cuts and fiscal stimulus. During

February and March, the administration’s focus was on the negative policy changes. More importantly, there is a great amount of uncertainty around his policies. Tariffs seem to be introduced and then cancelled at will, which negatively impacts consumer confidence as well as business confidence. Nobody will invest in a new car factory based on newly introduced tariffs on imported cars, if those tariffs can be cancelled a week later.

As a consequence, we have exited the most GDP sensitive businesses during the quarter. For instance, in our Q4 letter, we mentioned our investment in United Airlines (UAL). Our thesis was based on improving supply- demand dynamics within the US aviation market. After the first signs of travel bans for various government employees, we completely exited this position at a very good return for our investors.

We added significantly to our position in Distribution Solutions Group (DSGR), Mr. Cooper (COOP), AAR Corp (AIR) and DraftKings (DKNG). For all those investments, we expect significant earnings growth regardless of macro-economic conditions. They benefit from large inside ownership and have a track record of superior execution. They are our sleep-well names during volatile times.

Europe

During Q1, the German stock market appreciated by 18% after the Germans realized their economy has been held back by their fiscal discipline ever since the Great Financial Crisis. This was supplemented by the sudden belief in Europe that they have to invest more into their national defense. Increased government spending directly supports GDP growth which is supportive for European stocks. The market celebrated by buying into the DAX ETF, the main German index.

However, in typical European fashion, we expect the current spending plans to be watered down, and implementation to take longer than currently anticipated. Furthermore, some of the largest DAX constituents are SAP, Deutsche Telekom and Allianz, which are not necessarily the companies that will benefit the most from any fiscal spending. They’re also all inefficient conglomerates with poor historic track records of value creation.

Our European strategy has always been to focus on the small number of entrepreneurial companies with a clear future strategy. We avoid conglomerates.

We want our CEOs to dream about creating shareholder value. And when they succeed, we want them to become rich in the process. The average CEO of a European conglomerate dreams about stakeholders, but only between the hours of 9 to 5. That’s why our focus in Europe is even more on the management team and their alignment of interests. We prefer management teams who have coinvested with their shareholders and who stand to become extremely rich – but only if we get rich too.

Our largest positions in Europe remain Allfunds (ALLFG NA) and Haypp (HAYPP SS), both of which have been discussed in previous updates. As a reminder, Haypp is the dominant e-commerce platform for nicotine pouches such as Zyn. During Q4, various regulatory risks materialized at once. Despite those headwinds, and contrary to everyone’s expectations, Haypp reported its strongest quarter ever in Q4. Haypp’s business benefits from a more diverse customer base, and the dominance of Zyn was a net negative. Since last quarter, various new competing products to Zyn have come to market such as Velo Plus and Alp, and Haypp likely stands to benefit further. The magnitude of the improvement in profitability came as a surprise to us. We believe last quarter underscores the quality of both Haypp’s business model, as well as of its management team.

We are currently on our way to Sweden to attend Haypp’s Investor Day on April 3 rd and to learn more about their strategy for the next few years.

China

China has long been one of the cheapest markets in the world. China has gone through 5 years of deflation of its property bubble, which has caused most global investors to look elsewhere. What has largely gone unnoticed is the major transition the Chinese economy has undergone. The Chinese economy is no longer driven by the construction of ‘ghost towns’ or ‘bridges to nowhere’. Instead, the economy is driven by EV exports to Latin America, or the export of high-speed trains to Indonesia. This is best illustrated in the graph by our friends from Gavekal Research.

Since October 2024, China has introduced various measures to stimulate its domestic economy, such as subsidies when you trade in your phone or car. Such measures could take a while before they start impacting consumer confidence, but we believe that consumer spending has bottomed and 2025 will show a return to growth. Boosting the stock market also seems a clear priority. The CCP has introduced policies that allow national insurance companies to invest more in equities, and corporations can now borrow from local state banks to do buybacks. If Xi Jinping wants stock prices to go up, we want to own those stocks. Our focus remains on the Chinese consumer sector, as well as on companies that actively return capital to shareholders.

Investment Thesis: DPC Dash

Our largest Hong Kong listed investment is currently DPC Dash (OTCPK:DPCDF, 1405 HK), who owns the exclusive rights to operate Domino’s Pizza franchises in China, Hong Kong and Macau. We met with their management and 2 board members at the ICR conference in Orlando this January.

When I briefly lived in Beijing in 2008, the most popular Western pizza joint was Pizza Hut (owned by Yum China). At the time, Domino’s was largely absent in the Chinese market. This was because unlike Yum, Domino’s Pizza had never wanted to invest equity in its Chinese growth, preferring to rely fully on the franchise model. Therefore, Domino’s lacked the funding to hit critical mass and brand awareness.

This changed around 2017 when a new, local management team was hired and the parent Domino’s Pizza provided capital for expansion. DPC Dash has since grown to 1,000 stores. Importantly, since they exceeded the critical level of ~500 stores in 2021, they seem to have critical mass which results in the required brand awareness. Store count is currently growing 30 to 35% per year. The new management has localized the food offering (Durian Pizza) and to put it simply: Domino’s is hot in China. Several of the new stores have queues of up to 4 hours during their grand opening. New stores are often so overwhelmed by demand that they don’t do pizza deliveries in the first couple of months.

Let’s look at some simple math. A store costs roughly $200k to open. The average store does $100k in operating profit, meaning the payback is 2 years on average. However, when entering new cities, DPC has reported payback periods of less than a year. Companies that can reinvest capital at 50-100% ROIC are rare. We believe there is even some upside to the per-store profitability as DPC continues to grow and they will reap more scale benefits.

We bought our shares at a valuation of roughly $1.2m per store, or 12x store-level EBIT if we ignore corporate overhead. There are 5 other publicly traded Domino’s franchises. The worst performers (Poland, Saudi Arabia) trade at a valuation of $750k per store, but these geographies show no profitability (Poland) or negative growth (Saudi). The highest valuations are seen in India, where the valuation is $1.7m per store, even though Indian stores do half as much in revenue as Chinese stores and growth is significantly less.

More importantly, we see a realistic pathway for DPC Dash to grow from 1,000 stores in 2024 to 2,000 stores in 2027 and 3,000 stores in 2029, all organically funded. We believe 3,000 stores is achievable given that Pizza Hut owns roughly 3,600 stores and is still growing at 10%. This would bring the valuation down to $400k / store if we are willing to look out 5 years. By this simple math, DPC Dash has 3x to 5x potential if it continues to execute.

Conclusion

While the US economy is slowing and valuations are at high levels, both China and Europe are increasingly stimulating their economies and valuations are a lot more attractive. We believe our diversified exposure to the 3 regions provides meaningful downside protection in volatile times.

We intend to keep adding unique and attractive names to our portfolio. We are currently travelling to Sweden and Israel to meet with our existing portfolio holdings, as well as to source new investment ideas.

On behalf of the Night Watch team,

Roderick van Zuylen | Chief Investment Officer

Eileen Ke | Chief Operating Officer
