data.title: SharkNinja: Cooking A Compounder In The Long Term
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Modern Time Value Investing as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The Thesis

SharkNinja (NYSE:SN) is a well-known and loved brand and perceived as more valuable to consumers. The company is positioned to maintain its moat through a clear strategy and consumer-focused orientation. While the base-case scenario seems to provide a low-risk reward, I believe that the bull-case has a higher chance of materializing, and this scenario provides a margin of safety for value investors.

Company Background

Founded in 1997, SharkNinja is a global company known for designing and developing household appliances. The company first made its mark with the Shark vacuum cleaner in the 90s, and later broadened its product range by introducing the Ninja brand for kitchen and food preparation appliances in 2009. In 2017, the company was structured as a subsidiary of JS Global and IPOed on July 31, 2003. Before launching the Ninja brand in 2008, the company's sales were about $250 million. In 2024, company sales surpassed $5.5 billion, a 21% CAGR.

Business Model

The company's business model is quite simple - the company designs leading technology household appliances and sells them mostly through its distribution network, which mainly includes big retailers and e-commerce platforms (such as Walmart and Amazon, respectively). At the moment, the company has products in more than 36 subcategories of household appliances, spread in 4 main end markets -

From 2022 to 2024, SharkNinja has successfully reduced its reliance on cleaning appliances for revenue from 50% to 36%, showcasing its significant expansion into other product areas. This action aligns with one of the company's main strategic goals (which will be discussed below).

SharkNinja is outsourcing the manufacturing of 100% of its products to 3rd party manufacturers throughout Southeast Asia, as we can see in their presentation. After COVID-19 and the ongoing interruptions of the global supply chains, the company diversified its supplier base.

This decision has proven valuable, particularly amidst concerns regarding a potential US-China trade war and associated tariffs. By 2025, the company anticipates that over 90% of its products entering the US market will be unaffected by tariffs, thereby mitigating trade war risks. Another important point is that the manufacturing outside China has been outsourced at the same costs as inside China, as stated by SharkNinja's CEO at Morgan Stanley Global Consumer & Retail Conference:

So the product that we are producing today, outside of China and inside of China, is all at the same cost. It has always been our strategy that for our U.S. production, we wanted to move all of our manufacturing outside of China. We had set a goal for ourselves back a year ago that at the end of 2025, all of our U.S. production would be able to be made outside of China. We revisited that over the summer, and we made the decision to pull that forward. And so 90% of our U.S. production will be able to be made outside of China by the end of Q2.

Due to the fact that SharkNinja does not manufacture its products by itself, they are investing mainly in the design and engineering of its products. Thus, SharkNinja employs over 700 engineers out of its 3,000 employees, which are spread across the US, the UK, and China. The engineering headcount makes up about 25% of its workforce. This large engineering team significantly increases the company's research and development costs, which are roughly three times higher than the industry average. The big engineering teams allow the company to iterate its products to the consumer reactions and pain points faster than its competitors, which increases their customers' willingness to pay.

Source for industry numbers - Aswath Damodaran Database

Strategy

SharkNinja's strategy is based on 3 main principles:

Grow market share in existing categories that the company operates in, expand into new categories, and grow international footprint.

Growing Market Share in Existing Categories

This leg of the strategy is obvious and straightforward. The company wants to grow its market share inside the categories it already operates in, and these markets are mature (in terms of the company's presence).

As mentioned above, the company operates in 36 categories, which means it has a lot of room to grow its market share (in each category). The only way to do it is by developing and maintaining brand recognition and being perceived as valuable in the eyes of the customers.

Over the past 5 years, the company has managed to increase its market share among each of their existing categories significantly (in the US and the UK - their mature markets).Jumping S-Curves: Building a High-Performance Startup

This achievement is even more impressive when taking into consideration that the consumer expenditure on small household appliances' growth rate between 22-24 has been on a decline. This implies that the company is not only growing because of its entrance into new categories, but from its operations in existing categories and share-taking from competitors.

This achievement is attributed to the company's "Always-On Consumer" strategy, which involves continuous engagement with consumers to refine products and increase market penetration. A notable example is the modification of the flagship product, the Ninja Double, which was redesigned to be more compact and slenderer to cater to individuals with limited kitchen space. This strategic adjustment has likely contributed to an expanded consumer base and enhanced product appeal.

And it seems like that strategy comes to fruition. During a conversation with an expert in the retail industry in the UK, it was said as well:

"Yes, absolutely. Of course, I would. I think they've been actually a very disruptive but very positive story as far as I can see. I actually own some of the products. And I what stands out with SharkNinja is that it's driven by customers, and it's driven by product development as opposed to this is what we make, and we're going to continue making it and then market"

Expand into new categories

Expanding its product offering into new categories is incredibly important, as the company is constantly working on expanding its TAM. On its IPO filings in July 2023, the company cited 31 subcategories. At the end of 2024 (18 months later), the company is talking about 36 subcategories - a new subcategory every quarter! Expanding the TAM is important for the company to ensure a substantial growth rate in the future. According to the CEO's remarks at BofA Securities Consumer and Retail Conference 2025, this is the engine that allowed the company to increase its sales on CAGR by 21% over the last 20ish years.

So I started getting asked the same question when we did $400 million in revenue. And the caveat always was, well, the base is getting bigger. I mean, the base is pretty big right now. It's $5.5 billion. And what do I believe? I mean, you can cut our business in a number of different ways and look at it.

...

You can look at it across our three-pillar growth strategy. Our existing category growth, our new category expansion and international expansion. I think if you look at our business back in 2022, when the market was down 14% our existing categories declined a little, but we drove growth through new category expansion and international expansion.

As an investor, I find this point extremely important and encouraging. I love to see that the company has a long-term mindset and is preparing for the future. We can see how this dynamic works through an example of an S-Curve from an article called Jumping S-Curves: Building a High Performance Startup.

When a company is launching a product, the product adoption looks like an S curve -

When a product's growth is peaking, and the adoption reaches its limit of potential, the company has to come up with new products (or raise prices, which should be the last option, in my opinion). The optimal S-Curve should look like this -

This can be achieved when a company keeps on investing and on inventing itself, and that's why getting into new categories is one of the three pillars of SharkNinja's strategy, and I love seeing it as a guiding point.

Growing international footprint

SharkNinja, established in the late 1990s, initially focused on the domestic market in the United States. By 2014, it had entered the UK market and captured a significant share. And it was only in 2020 that SharkNinja decided to expand its direct operations to other European counters, with a primary focus on Germany and France.

The company anticipates that Germany and France could each generate $1 billion in revenue soon, exceeding the UK's estimated $900 million revenue in 2024.

With more than 80% of revenue coming from the US and the UK alone, it is not hard to understand the huge opportunity the company is facing in the future. In the same call at BofA Securities Consumer and Retail Conference 2025, the CEO also said that he sees the company's revenue mix as 50-50% in domestic and international. Moreover, the company's latest "hits" are not expected to reach other markets than the domestic only until later on this year because it can't keep up with the domestic demand.

"And I think in '24, we came out of the season with a number of home runs. I mean the SLUSHi, the Ninja SLUSHi -- was the Ninja Luxe Cafe, the Ninja Crispi, our Robot products did great. And so we had a lot of good momentum heading into 2025. And in fact, a lot of those '24 products only launched in the United States. So they won't be actually fully rolled out in the world until we get into really the third quarter and even the beginning of the fourth quarter of this year."

The company currently offers successful products that are well received by customers. Consequently, the time frame to achieve $1 billion in revenue in Germany and France is expected to be shorter compared to the UK and the US.

Competition

SharkNinja operates in a relatively mature market: electric household appliances. As shown above, the growth rate of household appliance expenditures in the US is relatively stable (excluding the COVID-19 effect in 2020-2021).

Despite that, SharkNinja has managed to grow its sales at a higher rate than its nearest competitors. The only way a company can manage to do that is with a moat that protects its economic "achievements" from competition (more on the moat below).

Not only is the growth impressive, but SharkNinja's ROIC is much higher than its peers -

In my opinion, it's not only the ROIC that is impressive, but the ROIIC (Return On Incremental Invested Capital) is growing, which means that the company has the ability to invest in higher return, which will keep driving growth forward!

SharkNinja is positioning itself as a "middle-price" company. Comparing its competition within leading categories, SharkNinja products are not the cheapest, but definitely not the most expensive ones. Despite the heavy investments in product development processes by the company.

Price alone does not provide a complete understanding. It is important to consider the price in relation to how customers perceive the product's value. A comparison of leading competitors on the Trustpilot review engine, focusing on UK reviews, indicates that SharkNinja is perceived as good and valuable for its prices. Putting the pieces together, we can conclude that while SharkNinja's products are not the cheapest out there, they bring the most value and are worth the price.

A similar conclusion can be drawn using quantitative analysis. Using Michael Mauboussin's work on cost leadership vs differentiation by decomposing the ROIC of each company, we can see that SharkNinja is in the area of both differentiation and price leadership, which means that the company is leading over its peers both through differentiation and lower costs. According to Mauboussin, one of the ways to achieve it is through sustaining a strong brand, and proprietary technology.

And it all goes back to the hefty investments in R&D and the process of iterations based on the customers' insights.

As mentioned above, for a company to be able to outgrow its peers, especially in a mature industry, it must have a moat. In the following chapter, I will try to assess the company's moat and understand how strong it is.

The Moat

I believe that SharkNinja holds a moat that can protect it from its competition. One of the reasons I believe so is because SharkNinja operates in a mature market with big, known competitors. Despite that, SharkNinja has managed to grow its revenue higher than the competition by wide margins.

I think that SharkNinja has a brand moat that resulted from reducing search costs by their customers (the customers see SharkNinja's products as good enough not to look for other products), and in turn, the company is investing heavily in increasing customers' willingness to pay.

Testing the Brand

There are several ways to assess the strength of the SharkNinja brand(s) moat, and I will go through some of it now.

Incremental Revenue to Sales & Marketing Spend

One of the ways to assess the brand is through the contribution to revenue of each dollar spent on marketing. The higher this metric, the more effective the marketing spend is, and we can assume that brand recognition is strong.

For SharkNinja, this metric has kept on getting better in the past couple of years. In a nutshell, that means that the marketing spend by the company is effective. The CEO also talked about it in a conference call -

"Look, we have 7x more social media engagement than our nearest competitor, 7x more engagement. And that means kind of likes and shares and repost and comments and things like that. We didn't spend a single dollar on the media. We've only launched our Ninja SLUSHi product in the United States. We didn't spend a dollar on media in the U.K., but we went, and we shipped in a couple of thousand units last week just to get a test of is there a demand in the market for the product. The 2,500 units sold out in 30 minutes. In 30 minutes, just by social media, hearing that the SLUSHi was available in the U.K."

This proves my point: the company's brand recognition is growing globally, and each marketing dollar generates increasing revenue. The brand recognition is so strong that the company does not need to advertise the new "hit products" directly, as happened with the SLUSHi product.

Google Trends as a Proxy to Interest

Another way to test SharkNinja's brand strength is through the interest and hype around new product launches and announcements. If the brand is strong, the later-launched products will have higher interest faster than older products.

Not surprisingly, this is exactly what is happening in SharkNinja's case. To prove my point, I gathered the company's product launches throughout 2024 (of new products only, not upgrades of old ones).

What we can see is that products that were launched later in the year got to 100 (the max interest on Google Trends) faster than products that were launched earlier that year. The conclusion that can be drawn here is connected to the point that the CEO was saying - there is a hype building around the company's product.

Social Media Presence

In the age of social media and influencers, brand moat (as an idea) is losing its credibility. Today, brands can reach audiences beyond comprehension through social media and influencers. Thus, to assess any brand, it is highly recommended to check its social media presence.

For Ninja's official Instagram brand page, the growth in the last 3 years in the follower’s count was 4.4x! and the growth is accelerating. This is the same dynamic that the CEO talked about and what we see in the numbers (every dollar spent on marketing derives higher revenues).

While encouraging, I think that there is another way to test the brand's social media presence, and this is through the hype around it. A notable example is the Ninja CREAMi home icemaker machine, introduced by the company in 2021. By 2023, not only will it have become the top seller in its category, but it will also contribute to a significant increase in the overall sales of the whole category.

The number of unofficial accounts for Ninja CREAMi recipes is astonishing, highlighting the strength of the company's brand. The official NinjaKitchen Instagram page boasts over 700K followers, while unofficial pages dedicated to Ninja CREAMi recipes also have a substantial number of followers.

Analyzing the hashtag activity of #NinjaCREAMI for the past 7 days shows substantial reach to more than 400K people!

To conclude the brand analysis, I think it is safe to say that SharkNinja holds a brand moat, and it is quite a strong one.

Risks

Although I believe the company possesses many commendable qualities, it is important to acknowledge that there are inherent risks associated with any business. Some of these risks are more evident, such as those related to the business cycle, economic downturns, and tariff uncertainties (although that one is mitigated, as shown above). Presently, these risks appear to be gaining prominence, and their likelihood of materializing seems to be increasing.

Here are the risks that, I think, should be considered:

  • Economic risks - With the emerging concerns for the economy, it is quite important to understand that SharkNinja is a company that falls under the category of discretionary purchases, the ones that are being cut at the beginning of an economic slowdown.
  • Customer Concentration - While SharkNinja is selling its products to end-users, its customers are usually retailers and e-commerce giants. As for the end of 2023, the three biggest customers of SharkNinja (Walmart, Amazon, and Costco) accounted for 44.7% of total sales. While this risk should not be mitigated, it is important to try and understand the company's relationship with its customers.

The first thing that would help us is the understanding that SharkNinja's products have some demand based on the brand (as concluded in the Moat section). The demand for the brand itself could give SharkNinja some bargaining power with its customers (Walmart would like to have these products on their shelves to draw more customers).

Rather than viewing this as a competitive relationship between two parties, it should be considered more of a collaborative "hand-in-hand" partnership. This perspective came to mind during the company's most recent call at BofA Securities Consumer and Retail Conference 2025:

"I mean, we're able to get daily POS down to the store level, down to the SKU level from every one of our major retailers. So, access to data is not a problem."

While this was an answer to another topic, I think that it does include some insights into SharkNinja's relationship with its major customers, and it is a kind of backing the claim of good relations.

Valuation

My valuation model is a DCF model, which is scenario-based. I used a DCF model not because of my faith that I can predict the future but as a guide and a framework to quantify my qualitative analysis and to try and factor scenarios to provide me with a "margin of safety" with the valuation.

In my base case, the CAGR I "predict" for the company in total revenues is 10.1%. I find it to be a reasonable CAGR, not only because of the quality of the business and the fact discussed above, but I also got the back of "statistics".

In his work on the base book effect, Michael Mauboussin provides CAGR statistics for companies within sectors. For consumer-facing companies (such as SharkNinja), the mean of the CAGR is 5.4%, with an SD of 8.6%. Without getting into statistics too much, what it means is that for that CAGR, I predicted to SharkNinja that the company only "needs to be better" than around 70% of the companies in that field. While not an easy task, the research and fundamentals discussed above seem to back that thesis (in my opinion).

In my base case, I assume that the growth rate in the North American region would decelerate; after all, the market is well penetrated by the company's product, and of course, there are concerns over consumer health. While I'm not a macro analyst, this is a scenario that needs to be considered in the models. I will note that this scenario is putting a little credit and faith that the company will be able to reinvent itself through new innovative product launches, as we saw happening during the last year. That is the main reason why I do believe the bull case scenario has higher chances to materialize.

Yet, the company is expected to grow in that case, where most of the growth would come mainly from Europe and from other international markets (Middle East, Asia, etc). This growth would require more working capital and more expenses. That is why my assumptions include slower margin expansion than in previous years (~300bp from 2022 to 2024 and ~300bp from 2024 to 2029) and a higher working capital in the short term.

For the discount rate, I used the WACC, a combination of cost of debt and equity. For the cost of equity, I used the CAPM model for the risk-free rate. I took the US 30-year yield, and for the equity premium, I chose to use Aswath Damodaran's implied equity risk premium. For the Beta, I took the simple average of the household appliances companies. For the cost of debt, I used implied interest through the interest expenses and the total debt of the company.

The bottom line for the valuation -

I also made a sensitivity analysis for the base case scenario, again, to create some margin of safety in the investment decision, but this time, it was based on factors that are beyond the company's control - terminal growth rate and the WACC.

As mentioned, this is a scenario-based model, and each scenario has its price target. Below are the targets according to each case scenario (bull, base, bear):

Summary

To sum it up, SharkNinja, as a company, seems to have passed the "moat" test. Throughout the analysis, I laid down the foundation of the strength of the company's moat and tested it in different ways. As a stock, it does seem like the recent downturn in the stock market as a whole and SN share prices are providing a convenient entry point, even if "only" the base case materializes. Personally, I do believe that there is a high chance of the bull case materializing, and in that case, there is quite a margin of safety.

data.title: Growth And Value: I Am Collecting Big Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Some debates will last for generations, like: Is a hot dog a hot sandwich?

Other debates, though, will be settled through future scientific discoveries. It never ceases to amaze me when we discover a new organ in the human body, an organ that has been present for generations and has only recently been discovered because of the advancements of our scientific ability to look at smaller and smaller pieces.

When it comes to the market, there have been two sides that have often waged an argumentative war against each other. This is the debate on whether it's better to invest in value stocks or invest in growth stocks. Typically, value stocks are those companies that are more mature, with smaller debt levels and larger cash piles. Historically, these are also dividend-paying companies because they reward their shareholders primarily through cash dividends or buybacks.

On the other side of the coin is growth investments. These are investments focused on growing the company, often running with negative cash flow, using cheap debt to fuel their growth. The goal is to become so large that eventually they become cash flow positive. Many of these growth investments either crash and burn into flames or provide massive, stunning returns. Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), and NVIDIA (NVDA) all qualify as historical growth stocks, some of which have turned into what many like to call "elder growth stocks," now that they are massive cash flow producing companies that use debt to fuel their growth.

Today, I want to look at a way to enjoy great income from both growth stocks and value stocks, letting you answer the question: Is it better to invest in growth or invest in value? The answer: if you're looking for great income, I have an option that allows you to collect great income from both sides of the coin.

Let's dive in!

Great Income From Value Stocks

Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), yielding 9.2%, is a CEF that focuses on small-cap stocks. It has consistently outperformed the Russell 2000 for an impressive 35 years. Source

While RVT and the Russell 2000 have joined in the recent market weakness, we expect that small-cap companies will outperform in the big picture. Source

Today, large-cap companies are trading at much higher valuations than small and mid-cap companies. The difference is the widest we have seen since 2000. While small-cap companies did see their valuations decline shortly before the recession in 2001, they dramatically outperformed once the recession started.

From 2000-2004, we saw a period where the S&P 500 was negative, but the Russell 2000 was positive. RVT significantly outperformed both.

We have a very similar starting point today, with large-caps trading at high valuations, while many small to mid-caps are trading at valuations normally seen when recessions are already underway.

RVT has gone through some changes, with founder Chuck Royce taking a step back from the day-to-day management to go into a well-deserved retirement. The management of the fund has been with Royce for a long time, with the "junior" member of the team being at Royce for 9 years. The rest have been with Royce for 17–30 years.

We can be confident that this management team is well-steeped in the Royce culture and are capable of executing in the footsteps of Chuck Royce.

RVT pays a variable dividend, which is calculated based upon the trailing four quarters of NAV. As RVT's NAV goes up and down, the distribution will as well, we provide an estimate of future dividends for the subscribers of High Dividend Opportunities.

RVT is a quick way to invest in small-cap companies, which are trading at very low valuations relative to large-cap companies. Historically, when these large gaps exist, the market eventually closes them. When this occurred in the early '00s, RVT went through a period of significant outperformance. As the valuation gap closes, we believe RVT is a great option to take advantage of that.

Great Income From Growth Stocks

BlackRock Science and Technology Trust, yielding 8.9%, is a CEF focused on Growth investments. NVIDIA and Microsoft are among its top holdings.

BST trades at a persistent discount to NAV, allowing you to gain exposure to growth investments while maximizing value per dollar invested:

This will allow an investor to enjoy exposure to growth investments without deciding when to buy or sell, abdicating that responsibility or that option to the portfolio managers. The real question, do the portfolio managers provide enough value versus the fees they charge to make this a worthwhile consideration, remains.

When we look over the long history of BST, we can see that the portfolio managers have provided exceptional value to their shareholders. While I'm not historically an advocate of selling shares to unlock cash, if you want to use a fund to gain exposure to massive growth investments that do not offer large dividends, something like BST can provide you with the option to do that and not have to worry about the timing of those actions. This is because fund managers are not emotionally invested in the situation will be able to do so from a much more objective position.

BST is likely to see more volatility in the near-term as growth investments see their premium values challenged, but historically, those same investments recover very strongly thereafter. This makes the CEF a good long-term investment through market cycles.

Conclusion

RVT and BST both provide phenomenal recurring income via distributions. They do this by adjusting and trimming their portfolio, collecting the dividends received from their holdings, and recycling capital into new investments. Both of these funds are very actively managed, meaning that the managers make decisions based upon their evaluation of the market and the companies in their holdings. These decisions can either make the fund more successful or make the fund lag in its performance. When compared to the market, we can see that both of these funds have a strong history of outperforming other options – including passive market ETFs (SPY). We can determine that these management teams provide true additional value to their shareholders. I'm not a big fan of having to trade in and out of holdings to try and unlock cash to live off of; however, I am willing to leverage the skills of others who have a positive track record doing that to pay me.

Retirement should be a time where you expend a ton of energy doing the things you were not able to do during your working life. For many, that means visiting family, traveling to see national parks, exploring other parts of the globe, or simply sitting at home in front of your fireplace with a hot cup of coffee and spending time with the ones you love the most. At the end of the day, most of us in our retirements don't want to be spending it chasing after different investments in the market or trying to time the market to unlock as much cash from each investment as possible. Should you invest in value or invest in growth for your retirement's income? The answer is both – by using these two funds, you can unlock income from both sides of the coin. Regardless of whether the economy is booming or busting, one of these two funds will always be consistently providing you with great income, if not both of them at the same time.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Why Multi-Theme Investing? Why GXDW?
data.description:
We’re living in a golden age of disruption, and thematic investing has become an indispensable tool for investors that seek to capitalize on structural change. By design, thematic strategies target long-term shifts, but their performance is often shaped by macroeconomic forces such as inflation, fiscal policy, capital cycles, interest rates, inventory cycles, or even geopolitics. While these factors may not alter the direction of the structural trend, they might often dictate short-term appetite for the strategy.

Multi-theme ETFs seek to simplify thematic investing by bundling multiple high-potential trends into a single strategy. But we believe a successful theme construction approach should go beyond backward-looking metrics like sales growth or earnings estimates to identify true disruptors. Moreover, a diversified basket of disruptive themes without the flexibility to adapt with the market’s direction can be self-defeating, offsetting winners with laggards and ultimately undermining performance.

In constructing multi-theme solutions, we believe a more dynamic approach is needed—one that captures emerging leadership as themes gain traction. We believe momentum indicators provide a disciplined framework for balancing market timing with thematic conviction. That’s why we have revamped our multi-theme offering, the Global X Thematic Growth ETF (previously traded under the ticker GXTG), and renaming it the Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW). GXDW seeks to track the Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Thematic RotationTM Total Return Index, which leverages Dorsey Wright’s proprietary Relative Strength methodology and dynamically rotates into the five themes with the strongest momentum across Global X’s Thematic ETF universe, offering investors a more agile way to access multiple themes through a single investment.

Key Takeaways

  • Disruption is accelerating, making thematic investing an essential tool for investors to stay agile and rapidly adapt.
  • Multi-theme strategies offer the power of thematic investing in a single strategy but need to evolve to balance disruptive potential with market reality.
  • Rotating into themes with higher momentum may be a more effective solution for multi-theme construction than earnings estimates or backward-looking metrics like sales growth.

Change Is the Only Constant

The modern economy is defined by accelerating disruption. Technology, demographic shifts, resource scarcity, and emerging forces like deglobalization are reshaping entire industries, while creating new investment opportunities. Most importantly, these structural shifts are converging in a way that amplifies their impact. AI is reshaping sectors like Healthcare, Financial Services, or even Media, energy is colliding with technology, and the digital and physical worlds are fusing, accelerating adoption curves and compelling corporations to rethink their investments. Innovation has never been linear, but today, its scale and speed are unprecedented.

Thematic investing offers a strategic approach that seeks to capitalize on these changes, isolating high-growth trends that may define the future. Ideally, thematic investing follows an unconstrained approach to capturing innovation, ignoring geographic boundaries, style biases, or traditional sector limitations. Market capitalization or size of a company should also not be restrictive factors. This broad, unconstrained approach can help gain exposure to the fastest growing and most rapidly adopted technologies.

Over the last 15 years, Global X has identified several of these powerful themes and developed targeted ETFs to provide investors with efficient access to companies benefitting from disruption. With the latest launches of the Global X U.S. Electrification ETF (ZAP), Global X Infrastructure Development ex-U.S. ETF (IPAV), and the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD), we now offer 32 ETFs that target high conviction, investable, long-term themes.1 At the industry level, U.S.-listed thematic ETFs managed nearly $90 billion in assets as of December 2024, a nearly fivefold increase over the past five years, which underscores the growing role of thematic strategies.2

But with over thirty mature themes recognized by Global X alone, and nearly 280 thematic ETFs available to investors in the U.S., in addition to other thematic solutions, selecting the right themes at the right time is becoming an increasingly arduous task.3

Simplifying Theme Selection

Multi-theme ETFs help investors navigate an increasingly crowded thematic landscape by providing exposure to multiple high-growth trends within a single investment. Their diversified structure not only simplifies theme selection but may also mitigates concentration risk in particular geographies, sectors, and market capitalizations. However, the effectiveness of these ETFs ultimately depends on how they are constructed.

We believe a key weakness in many multi-theme ETFs is their reliance on backward-looking metrics—such as sales growth or earnings—to select themes. While these fundamentals offer insight into long-term potential, they fail to capture shifting market dynamics or macroeconomic forces. Additionally, sales or earnings growth data typically emerges quarterly or semi-annually, while markets react far more swiftly. This lag may leave multi-theme ETFs exposed to themes facing short-term headwinds, potentially undermining performance.

Not All Themes Are Created Equal

While many themes are designed keeping a large total addressable market opportunity in mind, macroeconomic forces such as interest rates, fiscal policy, monetary policy, regulation, inflation, or even consumer behavior shifts can significantly influence the short-term trajectory of thematic trends, amplifying or dampening their momentum.

For instance, some themes like Electric Vehicles or Clean Technologies may require significant capital to become viable, meaning performance can lag in periods with high interest rates as the cost of funding innovation goes up. Similarly, themes like AI or Data Centers, reliant on large scale infrastructure investment, tend to favor well-capitalized incumbents with existing technological and computing advantages in early stages. Commodity-driven themes, such as Lithium, are highly sensitive to inventory cycles and fluctuating commodity prices. Themes such as Uranium and Nuclear Power can be limited by permitting cycles and regulatory factors.

Additionally, themes generally progress through distinct adoption cycles, often defined by the S-shaped adoption curve.4 Early stages of adoption are marked by experimentation and skepticism, while later stages see widespread integration, but also saturation risks. As a result, assessing a theme’s current positioning requires more than recognizing its disruptive potential or its immediate fundamental trajectory, and demands a deep understanding and recognition of market conditions and timing.

We believe investors should be selective, prioritizing themes that not only drive innovation but also align with favorable macroeconomic tailwinds. In our view, a multi-theme ETF that fails to account for these factors risks undermining long-term performance.

Balancing Innovation with Market Reality

The market constantly adapts, pricing in new realities. We believe well-constructed multi-theme solutions should in fact seek to leverage market movements to their advantage to understand what themes are positioned for outperformance relative to others.

Research suggests that investing when the market is scaling new heights is an effective long-term strategy, as strength often signals potential for outperformance.5 Winning themes tend to continue winning, further benefiting from capital inflows, increasing adoption, and strengthening narratives.6

Moreover, by systematically rotating into stronger themes and reducing exposure to relative underperformers, a market momentum-based strategy can exit poorly performing strategies, potentially helping the portfolio remain resilient during market downturns, which is key to the long-term success of a multi-theme ETF solution—where wild swings aren’t uncommon.

Multi-Theme Solution with Momentum at the Core

Ultimately, a superior multi-theme solution must balance disruptive potential with real-world market forces. With that framework in mind, we are revamping our multi-theme offering, transitioning from the Global X Thematic Growth ETF (GXTG) to the Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW), placing market momentum at the center of theme selection.

How GXDW Works

GXDW tracks the Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Thematic Rotation Total Return Index (NDWTR), which selects five thematic ETFs that demonstrate the strongest relative momentum in the markets. To qualify for inclusion, an ETF must be sponsored by Global X and focus on a specific, structurally disruptive theme or have a significant overweight toward a particular theme. Currently, the starting universe consists of 27 ETFs.7

To construct the index, Nasdaq Dorsey Wright applies its proprietary Nasdaq Dorsey Wright Relative Strength Matrix methodology, which seeks to rank ETFs in the starting universe based on their relative price momentum. Nasdaq Dorsey Wright uses Point and Figure charting to record supply and demand trends, filtering for insignificant trends and short-term market noise, focusing on capturing long-term price movements in the underlying ETFs. The top five ETFs with the strongest momentum are included in the index, seeking to track themes positioned for outperformance.

Key Methodology Highlights

  • Starting Universe: The starting universe consists of 27 thematic ETFs sponsored by Global X, which target a specific theme or that has a significant overweight toward a particular theme.
  • Momentum-Driven Selection: The Nasdaq Dorsey Wright method assigns a relative strength rank to each ETF in the starting universe, identifying the top five ranked ETFs for final inclusion in the index.
  • Equal Weighting: Selected ETFs receive an equal starting weight, providing balanced exposure to the most promising themes.
  • Rebalancing & Reconstitution: The index is evaluated monthly, with weighting adjustments made only when themes are added or removed, with the objective of allowing trends to develop without frequent turnover.

Momentum is a time-tested factor in investing. By focusing on sustained market strength while filtering out short-term fluctuations, GXDW provides investors with exposure to high-growth themes potentially poised for long-term outperformance.

Conclusion: GXDW Is a Multi-Theme Solution that Seeks to Harness Momentum

Thematic investing offers a powerful way to tap into structural changes reshaping the economy, but success lies in identifying the right themes at the right times. The Global X Dorsey Wright Thematic ETF (GXDW) leverages a momentum-based approach in an effort to dynamically capture the most promising disruptive trends, seeking to align investors with long-term growth drivers. By focusing on sustained market momentum, this strategy can help investors navigate the complexities of thematic investing, providing a disciplined, data-driven path to potentially capitalize on the next wave of transformative growth.
